title: Essential Tremor
oneLiner: Common, progressive, bilateral action tremor of hands/head/voice; often familial, benign in course but disabling in impact.

sections.Classic Vignette: A 72-year-old retired accountant presents to clinic because he can no longer sign his name legibly. He reports his hands have been “shaky” for about eight years, slowly worsening. He first noticed it when pouring tea. Both hands are affected equally. He volunteers that a couple of glasses of wine make the shaking “much better” for a few hours. There is no tremor at rest, no stiffness, slowness, or gait trouble. His father had “shaky hands” in old age. On examination, you ask him to hold his arms outstretched and a fine-to-coarse postural tremor appears immediately in both hands. When he performs finger-to-nose testing, the tremor amplifies as his finger approaches the target – a clear intention component. There is no rigidity, bradykinesia, or dystonic posturing. His gait is normal.
sections.Dissecting the Vignette:
- **Age 72, slowly progressive over years** — ET is age-related; peak onset 60–70, but can start at any age. Slow progression over decades is typical; rapid progression suggests another cause (e.g., cerebellar degeneration, drug-induced).
- **Both hands equally affected** — ET is almost always bilateral and symmetrical. Asymmetry should raise suspicion for Parkinson’s disease (PD) or a structural lesion.
- **Tremor on action (pouring, writing, holding arms outstretched)** — This is the hallmark: **postural** (sustained position) and **kinetic/action** (during movement). ET spares rest – if you see a resting tremor, think PD.
- **Improvement with alcohol** — This is a classic pattern in ET (up to 50–70% of patients report transient relief). Alcohol enhances GABA-A receptor activity, dampening cerebellar-thalamic oscillations. **Important:** this is **diagnostic** but **not a treatment** – don’t recommend it.
- **Family history** — ET is often autosomal dominant with incomplete penetrance. Up to 50% have a positive family history. If absent, consider sporadic ET or a mimic.
- **Intention component** — Unlike PD, ET *can* have intention worsening (terminal oscillation). This can mimic cerebellar tremor, but ET lacks other cerebellar signs (dysmetria, nystagmus, gait ataxia). The key distinction: ET is isolated tremor – no neurological soft signs.
- **No rigidity, bradykinesia, or gait disturbance** — This rules out PD and atypical parkinsonian syndromes. ET is a “pure tremor” disorder – other motor signs are absent.
sections.Atypical Presentations That May Trick Yo:
1. **Isolated head tremor** — “No-no” (horizontal) or “yes-yes” (vertical) tremor without hand involvement. This can be confused with cervical dystonia tremor. Key clue: dystonic tremor often has a directional component or a “null point” where tremor lessens; ET head tremor is more rhythmic and symmetrical.
2. **Voice tremor only** — A quivering, tremulous voice that worsens with sustained phonation. This may be misdiagnosed as spasmodic dysphonia. Listen for a rhythmic 4–8 Hz oscillation that disappears on whisper. Spasmodic dysphonia has strangled breaks, not rhythm.
3. **Kinetic tremor without postural component** — Especially in younger patients, the tremor may only appear during fine motor tasks (e.g., threading a needle) and be absent on simple arm extension. This can be labelled “physiologic tremor” or “anxiety tremor.” Clues: longstanding, stable, alcohol-responsive, family history.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Essential tremor is a chronic, slowly progressive disorder of movement that causes a rhythmic, shaking motion of the hands (and sometimes head, voice, legs, or trunk) during voluntary actions such as holding a cup, writing, or eating. It is not life-threatening but can be severely disabling and socially embarrassing. It is the most common movement disorder.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Frequency:** ET affects 1–5% of the general population, rising to 5–10% >60 years old. You will see it many times.
- **Misdiagnosis:** ET is frequently mistaken for early Parkinson’s disease (PD). This leads to inappropriate treatment, unnecessary anxiety, and incorrect counselling – e.g., prescribing levodopa to a patient with ET is not only ineffective but may cause side effects, and mislabelling a patient as having PD has enormous psychosocial consequences.
- **Disability:** Up to 60% of patients report functional impairment in daily activities (eating, drinking, writing). It also increases fall risk due to intention tremor.
- **Treatment gap:** Despite being common, many patients are never offered treatment because clinicians assume it is “normal aging” or “benign.” We have effective first-line therapies.
- **Cognitive link:** Emerging evidence suggests ET patients have a mildly increased risk of dementia (odds ratio ~1.5), possibly due to cerebellar-cortical network degeneration. This is not “benign” in the long term.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story:** Essential tremor is thought to arise from abnormal oscillatory activity in the **cerebello-thalamo-cortical (CTC) loop**. The cerebellum, normally a fine-tuner of movement, begins to generate rhythmic bursts of activity. These bursts travel via the thalamus to the motor cortex, and then down to the spinal cord, producing a tremor in the 4–12 Hz range. The exact cellular culprit is debated, but converging evidence points to **Purkinje cell dysfunction** and **GABAergic dysregulation** in the cerebellar cortex.

**The mechanism → sign chain:**

- **Purkinje cell loss** (seen in post-mortem studies of ET) → reduced GABA output from cerebellar cortex to deep cerebellar nuclei (DCN) → DCN become hyperexcitable → increased oscillatory drive to thalamus → tremor.
- **GABA-A receptor dysfunction** may also occur in the thalamus. This explains why **alcohol** (a GABA-A agonist) and **benzodiazepines** (also GABA-A positive allosteric modulators) suppress tremor. It also explains why **primidone** (converted to phenobarbital, which potentiates GABA) works.
- **Why postural/kinetic and not rest?** In ET, the oscillation is “turned on” by voluntary movement or sustained posture, not by dopamine deficiency. The CTC loop is normally quiescent at rest; it activates when you intend to hold still or move. In contrast, Parkinson’s tremor is driven by pathological activity in the **basal ganglia-thalamo-cortical loop** (dopamine-dependent), which fires even at rest and dampens with action.

**Connecting to the vignette:**

- Bilateral symmetrical tremor → the CTC loop is paired in both hemispheres; ET is a “network” disorder, not a focal lesion.
- Alcohol response → GABA agonism dampens the oscillation at the cerebellar or thalamic level.
- Intentional worsening → terminal oscillation is a classic sign of cerebellar system involvement. The cerebellum decodes the “endpoint” of movement; when its output is oscillatory, the finger overshoots and corrects rhythmically.
- No bradykinesia or rigidity → these require dopamine deficiency in the nigrostriatal pathway, which is intact in ET. This is the crucial point: ET is a **cerebellar** disorder, not a **basal ganglia** disorder.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|---|---|---|
| **Idiopathic (most common ~50%)** | No identifiable trigger. Diagnosis of exclusion. | Long-standing, slowly progressive, no other neurological signs. |
| **Genetic / Familial** | Autosomal dominant (ETM1 on 3q13, ETM2 on 2p25, others). | “My father and grandfather both had shaky hands.” |
| **Medication-induced** | Valproate, lithium, beta-agonists, theophylline, TCAs, SSRIs, corticosteroids, thyroxine, amiodarone. | “Tremor started a few weeks after starting a new medicine.” |
| **Metabolic / Endocrine** | Hyperthyroidism, hypoglycaemia, hepatic encephalopathy (asterixis – more flapping), uraemia, Wilson’s disease (but this is more dystonic/choreic). | Weight loss, heat intolerance (hyperthyroidism); asterixis (liver disease). |
| **Toxic** | Alcohol withdrawal, heavy metal poisoning (mercury, lead, arsenic), toluene. | “He stopped drinking three days ago” (delirium tremens). |
| **Enhanced physiologic tremor** | Anxiety, fatigue, caffeine, fever, sleep deprivation. | “Only happens when I’m stressed” – fine 8–12 Hz, usually reversible. |

**Most common cause:** **Idiopathic** – no identifiable underlying trigger. However, always check for drug-induced and hyperthyroidism because they are reversible.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M: There is **no diagnostic test** for essential tremor. The diagnosis is **clinical**, based on history and exam. However, you must rule out mimics.
sections.Diagnostic Criteria (Tremor Research Gro:
| Criterion | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Inclusion** | Bilateral, largely symmetrical, postural or kinetic tremor of hands and forearms. May also involve head, voice, legs, trunk (but not  isolated – see note below). Duration ≥ 3 years. |
| **Exclusion** | Rest tremor; rigidity; bradykinesia; dystonia; cerebellar signs (ataxia, dysmetria); neuropathic tremor; psychogenic tremor; known cause (drugs, hyperthyroidism). |

**Pattern recognition:** A single finding that **confirms** ET: Bilateral action tremor of hands with **normal** neurological exam (no bradykinesia, rigidity, or ataxia) that improves with alcohol. A single finding that **rules out** ET: **rest tremor** – until proven otherwise, this is Parkinson’s disease.
sections.Tests to Order (When to Use):
| Test | Why | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **TSH / Free T4** | Rule out hyperthyroidism – a reversible cause. | TSH < 0.4 mIU/L + high T4 = hyperthyroid tremor. Treat the underlying thyroid disorder. |
| **Serum copper + ceruloplasmin** | Only if < 40 years old or atypical features (dystonia, dysarthria, Kayser-Fleischer rings). | Low ceruloplasmin and copper – think Wilson’s disease (tremor here is often wing-beating, not classic ET). |
| **Liver enzymes + ammonia** | If asterixis or hepatic flavour (jaundice, ascites). | Elevated AST/ALT + high ammonia = hepatic tremor. Manage liver disease. |
| **Brain MRI** | Only if asymmetrical, >1 neurological sign, rapid progression, or “red flags” (e.g., stroke-like onset). | Normal in ET. Lesions in cerebellum, brainstem, or thalamus suggest structural tremor (e.g., stroke, MS, tumour). |
| **Serum drug levels** | If suspect toxicity (lithium, valproate). | Correct levels or change dose. |
| **Electrophysiology** | Surface EMG accelerometry – rarely needed in routine practice; can confirm tremor frequency and distinguish from psychogenic (entrainment). | EMG bursts alternating (agonist/antagonist) at 4–12 Hz. Entrainment test positive in psychogenic tremor. |

**Key message:** If the history and exam are classic (bilateral, action, alcohol-responsive, family history, no other signs), **no tests are needed**. Just counsel and begin treatment. Over-investigation is a common mistake.

---
sections.Immediate (First Hour):
- **No acute emergency.** Essential tremor does not present as a crisis. However, if a patient has **new, sudden-onset tremor** with neurological deficits, treat as stroke/TIA until proven otherwise.
- If the patient presents with severe tremor causing falls or inability to eat/drink, consider **propranolol 40 mg PO once** as a test dose (watch for bradycardia, asthma exacerbation). This is not standard; most treatment is outpatient.
sections.Ward / Inpatient (24–72 hours):
- You rarely admit a patient for ET alone. If admitted for another reason and you diagnose ET:
  - **Start first-line treatment** if tremor is causing functional impairment.
  - **First-line:** **Propranolol** (non-selective beta-blocker) – start **40 mg PO twice daily**, titrate up to 320 mg/day divided. Effect seen in 1–2 weeks. Mechanism: blocks peripheral beta-2 receptors (reduces muscle spindle sensitivity) and central effects.
  - **Second-line:** **Primidone** (barbiturate prodrug) – start **50 mg at bedtime** (very sedating), increase slowly to 250–500 mg/day divided. Caution: first dose may cause severe sedation and vertigo – warn patients.
  - **Avoid:** **Anticholinergics** (benztropine, trihexyphenidyl) – they are for Parkinson’s tremor, not ET, and cause confusion, dry mouth, constipation.
  - **Avoid:** **Levodopa** – always a pitfall. If you misdiagnose ET as PD, your patient will not improve.
sections.Long-Term:
- **Beta-blockers (propranolol)** remain first-line. Alternative beta-blocker: **atenolol 50–100 mg daily** (if asthma or COPD – propranolol is contraindicated in asthma). Atenolol is cardioselective; may be less effective but safer.
- **Primidone** if beta-blockers fail or are contraindicated.
- **Topiramate** – 25 mg daily, titrate up to 100–400 mg/day. Careful with side effects: cognitive dulling, word-finding difficulty, paraesthesia, weight loss, acute angle-closure glaucoma risk.
- **Gabapentin** – 300 mg TID to 1200 mg TID. Less evidence, but may be useful for head/voice tremor.
- **Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS)** – **Ventral intermediate nucleus (VIM) of the thalamus** is the target. Indicated for severe, medication-refractory ET. Most effective for hand tremor; less so for head/voice. Risk of dysarthria, ataxia.
- **Benzodiazepines** (clonazepam, alprazolam) – use sparingly due to tolerance and addiction risk. Only for short-term relief (e.g., before a social event).
- **Botulinum toxin A** – injections into forearm muscles for hand tremor, into neck muscles for head tremor. Evidence moderate; risk of focal weakness.
- **Alcohol** – Never prescribe it. Patients report it, but it carries addiction, sedation, and hangover effects. Use as a *diagnostic clue*, not a treatment.
sections.What NOT to Do (Common Pitfalls):
- **Don’t call it “benign essential tremor”** — it can be severely disabling.
- **Don’t use propranolol in asthma** — can cause fatal bronchospasm.
- **Don’t start primidone at high dose** — first-dose phenomenon (confusion, ataxia, vomiting) is common; start 50 mg nocte.
- **Don’t use levodopa** — it won’t work and delays appropriate therapy.
- **Don’t undertake extensive imaging** for a classic presentation.
- **Don’t forget to ask about family history and alcohol effect** — these are the strongest pattern-recognition cues.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Essential Tremor | Parkinson’s Disease | Dystonic Tremor | Enhanced Physiologic Tremor | Cerebellar Tremor |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|------------------|-----------------------------|-------------------|
| **Pain** | None | Only if rigidity (aching) or dystonia | Often painful (cervical dystonia) | None | None |
| **Exam** | Action tremor only; no bradykinesia, rigidity, or gait disturbance | Rest tremor (pill-rolling), bradykinesia, rigidity, shuffling gait | Focal tremor at 6–8 Hz; directional (null point); dystonic posturing (head tilt, torticollis) | Fine 8–12 Hz postural; other signs of anxiety (tachycardia, hyperhidrosis) | Intention tremor (finger-to-nose worsens near target); dysmetria, ataxic gait, nystagmus, scanning speech |
| **ECG** | Normal (unless beta-blocker effects) | Normal | Normal | Sinus tachycardia | Normal |
| **Labs** | All normal | Normal | Normal | TSH, electrolytes normal; consider cortisol, caffeine | MRI shows cerebellar lesion (stroke, MS, tumour) |
| **Key Diff** | **Improves with alcohol** | No alcohol improvement; dopamine deficiency on DaTSCAN | May improve with touching the “null point” (e.g., placing a finger on the chin reduces head tremor) | Reversible when trigger removed | Structural lesion on imaging; other cerebellar signs |
| **Tremor Type** | Postural + kinetic (4–12 Hz) | Resting (3–6 Hz), but can have re-emergent postural tremor | Variable – often task-specific (e.g., writer’s cramp) | Postural (fine) | Intention (3–5 Hz) |

**Memory aid:** Think “**PARDON**” — **P**arkinson (rest), **A**ction (essential), **R**eversible (physiologic), **D**ystonic (directional), **O**ther signs (cerebellar with ataxia), **N**ormal exam (essential is pure tremor).

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Missed diagnosis leads to:**

1. **Functional disability** – inability to eat without spilling, write legibly, use tools. Leads to social withdrawal and loss of independence. **What to do:** Screen for functional impact with simple questions: “Can you drink from a cup without spilling? Can you sign your name?” Offer treatment.
2. **Falls** – Intention tremor can cause loss of balance when reaching for objects, especially climbing stairs or carrying items. Secondary fractures. **What to do:** Assess fall risk (get up and go test), refer to physio for balance exercises.
3. **Depression and social anxiety** – Embarrassment leads to avoidance of public eating/drinking. **What to do:** Ask about mood; refer to psychology; consider SSRIs (but be aware they can worsen tremor in some patients).
4. **Misdiagnosis as Parkinson’s disease** – Receiving inappropriate levodopa can cause side effects (nausea, dyskinesia, hallucinations in elderly) and delay correct management. Emotional harm of being told you have a neurodegenerative disease. **What to do:** Always insist on the presence of bradykinesia or rigidity before labelling PD. If uncertain, refer to movement disorder specialist.
5. **Late-stage cognitive decline** – Long-term studies show a slightly increased risk of dementia (OR ~1.5–2). Unlike PD, dementia is not a core feature, but cerebellar cognitive affective syndrome (CCAS) can occur. **Pattern:** executive dysfunction, verbal fluency problems, emotional lability. **What to do:** Screen with MoCA; refer to neurology if cognitive complaints.

---
sections.Mnemonics:
1. **“ET: Exaggerated Tremor of the Arms – Check Head & Voice”** — reminds you that head and voice involvement are supportive of ET (though not required). If head tremor is the only manifestation, consider dystonia.

2. **“A-B-C-D”** for differentiating ET vs Parkinson’s:
   - **A**ction | **B**ilateral | **C**oarse (intention) → **E**ssential
   - **A**kinesia (bradykinesia) | **B**ent posture | **C**ogwheel rigidity | **D**ecreased arm swing → Parkinson’s

3. **“ALCOHOL IMPROVES”** – Not a mnemonic, but the most valuable pattern-recognition phrase to teach juniors. If you hear this, think ET until proven otherwise.
sections.Clinical Pearls (Pattern-Recognition Cue:
1. **“If the tremor stops when the patient is distracted, it’s psychogenic.”** True psychogenic tremor shows entrainment (tremor frequency matches another repetitive movement, e.g., tapping). ET does not entrain.
2. **“A patient with hand tremor who can still write a straight line with barely a wobble probably has Parkinson’s, not ET.”** PD handwriting is small (micrographia) but not tremulous; ET handwriting is large, shaky, and jagged.
3. **“The ‘water test’:** Ask the patient to carry a full cup of water across the room. In ET, water spills. In PD, they may lean forward, take short steps, and the cup is stable at rest – but they may spill due to freezing.
4. **“Propranolol works better for hand than head/voice tremor.”** For head and voice, consider primidone or botulinum toxin.
5. **“Isolated chin tremor in young adults is usually physiologic or essential, but never put a label on it until you’ve seen a neurologist.”** Reassure but review.
6. **“Don’t screen for Wilson’s disease in every patient >40 years old with tremor.”** Wilson’s presents before age 30; ceruloplasmin and copper testing outside this age is low yield unless other signs present.
7. **“If tremor is unilateral, think structural lesion first, until proven otherwise.”** Do brain MRI before labelling as ET.
sections.Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:
- **Calling early PD “essential tremor”** — missing bradykinesia (e.g., reduced arm swing, hypomimia) is the most common error. Always check rapid finger tapping, foot tapping, and gait.
- **Prescribing propranolol without checking heart rate and asthma history** — can cause syncope, bronchospasm. Get baseline ECG if >65 years or history of cardiac disease.
- **Dismissing tremor as “just stress”** — older adults often present late; they may have suffered for years. Take it seriously.
- **Starting primidone at 250 mg daily** — leads to “first-dose phenomenon” (severe dizziness, ataxia). Start low (50 mg nocte) and go slow.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria: Essential tremor is not a medical emergency. However, you need to know when the situation is **not** simple ET.
sections.When to Call Senior / On-Call Neurologis:
- **Rapid onset** (days to weeks) — possible stroke, demyelination, drug toxicity, or psychogenic.
- **New neurological signs** – e.g., hemiparesis, ataxia, dysarthria, nystagmus, visual field deficits. This could be cerebellar stroke or tumour.
- **Rest tremor** + bradykinesia or rigidity – suggests Parkinson’s disease; refer to neurologist for initiation of dopamine therapy.
- **Tremor causing acute injury** – e.g., fall with head strike. Manage fall; then address tremor long-term.
- **Patient on anticoagulation with new head tremor** – consider cerebellar haemorrhage; urgent CT head.

rawMarkdown:
# Essential Tremor

**One-liner:** Common, progressive, bilateral action tremor of hands/head/voice; often familial, benign in course but disabling in impact.

---

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You’ll See

### Classic Vignette
A 72-year-old retired accountant presents to clinic because he can no longer sign his name legibly. He reports his hands have been “shaky” for about eight years, slowly worsening. He first noticed it when pouring tea. Both hands are affected equally. He volunteers that a couple of glasses of wine make the shaking “much better” for a few hours. There is no tremor at rest, no stiffness, slowness, or gait trouble. His father had “shaky hands” in old age. On examination, you ask him to hold his arms outstretched and a fine-to-coarse postural tremor appears immediately in both hands. When he performs finger-to-nose testing, the tremor amplifies as his finger approaches the target – a clear intention component. There is no rigidity, bradykinesia, or dystonic posturing. His gait is normal.

### Dissecting the Vignette

- **Age 72, slowly progressive over years** — ET is age-related; peak onset 60–70, but can start at any age. Slow progression over decades is typical; rapid progression suggests another cause (e.g., cerebellar degeneration, drug-induced).
- **Both hands equally affected** — ET is almost always bilateral and symmetrical. Asymmetry should raise suspicion for Parkinson’s disease (PD) or a structural lesion.
- **Tremor on action (pouring, writing, holding arms outstretched)** — This is the hallmark: **postural** (sustained position) and **kinetic/action** (during movement). ET spares rest – if you see a resting tremor, think PD.
- **Improvement with alcohol** — This is a classic pattern in ET (up to 50–70% of patients report transient relief). Alcohol enhances GABA-A receptor activity, dampening cerebellar-thalamic oscillations. **Important:** this is **diagnostic** but **not a treatment** – don’t recommend it.
- **Family history** — ET is often autosomal dominant with incomplete penetrance. Up to 50% have a positive family history. If absent, consider sporadic ET or a mimic.
- **Intention component** — Unlike PD, ET *can* have intention worsening (terminal oscillation). This can mimic cerebellar tremor, but ET lacks other cerebellar signs (dysmetria, nystagmus, gait ataxia). The key distinction: ET is isolated tremor – no neurological soft signs.
- **No rigidity, bradykinesia, or gait disturbance** — This rules out PD and atypical parkinsonian syndromes. ET is a “pure tremor” disorder – other motor signs are absent.

### Atypical Presentations That May Trick You

1. **Isolated head tremor** — “No-no” (horizontal) or “yes-yes” (vertical) tremor without hand involvement. This can be confused with cervical dystonia tremor. Key clue: dystonic tremor often has a directional component or a “null point” where tremor lessens; ET head tremor is more rhythmic and symmetrical.
2. **Voice tremor only** — A quivering, tremulous voice that worsens with sustained phonation. This may be misdiagnosed as spasmodic dysphonia. Listen for a rhythmic 4–8 Hz oscillation that disappears on whisper. Spasmodic dysphonia has strangled breaks, not rhythm.
3. **Kinetic tremor without postural component** — Especially in younger patients, the tremor may only appear during fine motor tasks (e.g., threading a needle) and be absent on simple arm extension. This can be labelled “physiologic tremor” or “anxiety tremor.” Clues: longstanding, stable, alcohol-responsive, family history.

---

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:** Essential tremor is a chronic, slowly progressive disorder of movement that causes a rhythmic, shaking motion of the hands (and sometimes head, voice, legs, or trunk) during voluntary actions such as holding a cup, writing, or eating. It is not life-threatening but can be severely disabling and socially embarrassing. It is the most common movement disorder.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Frequency:** ET affects 1–5% of the general population, rising to 5–10% >60 years old. You will see it many times.
- **Misdiagnosis:** ET is frequently mistaken for early Parkinson’s disease (PD). This leads to inappropriate treatment, unnecessary anxiety, and incorrect counselling – e.g., prescribing levodopa to a patient with ET is not only ineffective but may cause side effects, and mislabelling a patient as having PD has enormous psychosocial consequences.
- **Disability:** Up to 60% of patients report functional impairment in daily activities (eating, drinking, writing). It also increases fall risk due to intention tremor.
- **Treatment gap:** Despite being common, many patients are never offered treatment because clinicians assume it is “normal aging” or “benign.” We have effective first-line therapies.
- **Cognitive link:** Emerging evidence suggests ET patients have a mildly increased risk of dementia (odds ratio ~1.5), possibly due to cerebellar-cortical network degeneration. This is not “benign” in the long term.

---

## 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story:** Essential tremor is thought to arise from abnormal oscillatory activity in the **cerebello-thalamo-cortical (CTC) loop**. The cerebellum, normally a fine-tuner of movement, begins to generate rhythmic bursts of activity. These bursts travel via the thalamus to the motor cortex, and then down to the spinal cord, producing a tremor in the 4–12 Hz range. The exact cellular culprit is debated, but converging evidence points to **Purkinje cell dysfunction** and **GABAergic dysregulation** in the cerebellar cortex.

**The mechanism → sign chain:**

- **Purkinje cell loss** (seen in post-mortem studies of ET) → reduced GABA output from cerebellar cortex to deep cerebellar nuclei (DCN) → DCN become hyperexcitable → increased oscillatory drive to thalamus → tremor.
- **GABA-A receptor dysfunction** may also occur in the thalamus. This explains why **alcohol** (a GABA-A agonist) and **benzodiazepines** (also GABA-A positive allosteric modulators) suppress tremor. It also explains why **primidone** (converted to phenobarbital, which potentiates GABA) works.
- **Why postural/kinetic and not rest?** In ET, the oscillation is “turned on” by voluntary movement or sustained posture, not by dopamine deficiency. The CTC loop is normally quiescent at rest; it activates when you intend to hold still or move. In contrast, Parkinson’s tremor is driven by pathological activity in the **basal ganglia-thalamo-cortical loop** (dopamine-dependent), which fires even at rest and dampens with action.

**Connecting to the vignette:**

- Bilateral symmetrical tremor → the CTC loop is paired in both hemispheres; ET is a “network” disorder, not a focal lesion.
- Alcohol response → GABA agonism dampens the oscillation at the cerebellar or thalamic level.
- Intentional worsening → terminal oscillation is a classic sign of cerebellar system involvement. The cerebellum decodes the “endpoint” of movement; when its output is oscillatory, the finger overshoots and corrects rhythmically.
- No bradykinesia or rigidity → these require dopamine deficiency in the nigrostriatal pathway, which is intact in ET. This is the crucial point: ET is a **cerebellar** disorder, not a **basal ganglia** disorder.

---

## 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|---|---|---|
| **Idiopathic (most common ~50%)** | No identifiable trigger. Diagnosis of exclusion. | Long-standing, slowly progressive, no other neurological signs. |
| **Genetic / Familial** | Autosomal dominant (ETM1 on 3q13, ETM2 on 2p25, others). | “My father and grandfather both had shaky hands.” |
| **Medication-induced** | Valproate, lithium, beta-agonists, theophylline, TCAs, SSRIs, corticosteroids, thyroxine, amiodarone. | “Tremor started a few weeks after starting a new medicine.” |
| **Metabolic / Endocrine** | Hyperthyroidism, hypoglycaemia, hepatic encephalopathy (asterixis – more flapping), uraemia, Wilson’s disease (but this is more dystonic/choreic). | Weight loss, heat intolerance (hyperthyroidism); asterixis (liver disease). |
| **Toxic** | Alcohol withdrawal, heavy metal poisoning (mercury, lead, arsenic), toluene. | “He stopped drinking three days ago” (delirium tremens). |
| **Enhanced physiologic tremor** | Anxiety, fatigue, caffeine, fever, sleep deprivation. | “Only happens when I’m stressed” – fine 8–12 Hz, usually reversible. |

**Most common cause:** **Idiopathic** – no identifiable underlying trigger. However, always check for drug-induced and hyperthyroidism because they are reversible.

---

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

There is **no diagnostic test** for essential tremor. The diagnosis is **clinical**, based on history and exam. However, you must rule out mimics.

### Diagnostic Criteria (Tremor Research Group 2018)

| Criterion | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Inclusion** | Bilateral, largely symmetrical, postural or kinetic tremor of hands and forearms. May also involve head, voice, legs, trunk (but not  isolated – see note below). Duration ≥ 3 years. |
| **Exclusion** | Rest tremor; rigidity; bradykinesia; dystonia; cerebellar signs (ataxia, dysmetria); neuropathic tremor; psychogenic tremor; known cause (drugs, hyperthyroidism). |

**Pattern recognition:** A single finding that **confirms** ET: Bilateral action tremor of hands with **normal** neurological exam (no bradykinesia, rigidity, or ataxia) that improves with alcohol. A single finding that **rules out** ET: **rest tremor** – until proven otherwise, this is Parkinson’s disease.

### Tests to Order (When to Use)

| Test | Why | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **TSH / Free T4** | Rule out hyperthyroidism – a reversible cause. | TSH < 0.4 mIU/L + high T4 = hyperthyroid tremor. Treat the underlying thyroid disorder. |
| **Serum copper + ceruloplasmin** | Only if < 40 years old or atypical features (dystonia, dysarthria, Kayser-Fleischer rings). | Low ceruloplasmin and copper – think Wilson’s disease (tremor here is often wing-beating, not classic ET). |
| **Liver enzymes + ammonia** | If asterixis or hepatic flavour (jaundice, ascites). | Elevated AST/ALT + high ammonia = hepatic tremor. Manage liver disease. |
| **Brain MRI** | Only if asymmetrical, >1 neurological sign, rapid progression, or “red flags” (e.g., stroke-like onset). | Normal in ET. Lesions in cerebellum, brainstem, or thalamus suggest structural tremor (e.g., stroke, MS, tumour). |
| **Serum drug levels** | If suspect toxicity (lithium, valproate). | Correct levels or change dose. |
| **Electrophysiology** | Surface EMG accelerometry – rarely needed in routine practice; can confirm tremor frequency and distinguish from psychogenic (entrainment). | EMG bursts alternating (agonist/antagonist) at 4–12 Hz. Entrainment test positive in psychogenic tremor. |

**Key message:** If the history and exam are classic (bilateral, action, alcohol-responsive, family history, no other signs), **no tests are needed**. Just counsel and begin treatment. Over-investigation is a common mistake.

---

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

### Immediate (First Hour)
- **No acute emergency.** Essential tremor does not present as a crisis. However, if a patient has **new, sudden-onset tremor** with neurological deficits, treat as stroke/TIA until proven otherwise.
- If the patient presents with severe tremor causing falls or inability to eat/drink, consider **propranolol 40 mg PO once** as a test dose (watch for bradycardia, asthma exacerbation). This is not standard; most treatment is outpatient.

### Ward / Inpatient (24–72 hours)
- You rarely admit a patient for ET alone. If admitted for another reason and you diagnose ET:
  - **Start first-line treatment** if tremor is causing functional impairment.
  - **First-line:** **Propranolol** (non-selective beta-blocker) – start **40 mg PO twice daily**, titrate up to 320 mg/day divided. Effect seen in 1–2 weeks. Mechanism: blocks peripheral beta-2 receptors (reduces muscle spindle sensitivity) and central effects.
  - **Second-line:** **Primidone** (barbiturate prodrug) – start **50 mg at bedtime** (very sedating), increase slowly to 250–500 mg/day divided. Caution: first dose may cause severe sedation and vertigo – warn patients.
  - **Avoid:** **Anticholinergics** (benztropine, trihexyphenidyl) – they are for Parkinson’s tremor, not ET, and cause confusion, dry mouth, constipation.
  - **Avoid:** **Levodopa** – always a pitfall. If you misdiagnose ET as PD, your patient will not improve.

### Long-Term
- **Beta-blockers (propranolol)** remain first-line. Alternative beta-blocker: **atenolol 50–100 mg daily** (if asthma or COPD – propranolol is contraindicated in asthma). Atenolol is cardioselective; may be less effective but safer.
- **Primidone** if beta-blockers fail or are contraindicated.
- **Topiramate** – 25 mg daily, titrate up to 100–400 mg/day. Careful with side effects: cognitive dulling, word-finding difficulty, paraesthesia, weight loss, acute angle-closure glaucoma risk.
- **Gabapentin** – 300 mg TID to 1200 mg TID. Less evidence, but may be useful for head/voice tremor.
- **Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS)** – **Ventral intermediate nucleus (VIM) of the thalamus** is the target. Indicated for severe, medication-refractory ET. Most effective for hand tremor; less so for head/voice. Risk of dysarthria, ataxia.
- **Benzodiazepines** (clonazepam, alprazolam) – use sparingly due to tolerance and addiction risk. Only for short-term relief (e.g., before a social event).
- **Botulinum toxin A** – injections into forearm muscles for hand tremor, into neck muscles for head tremor. Evidence moderate; risk of focal weakness.
- **Alcohol** – Never prescribe it. Patients report it, but it carries addiction, sedation, and hangover effects. Use as a *diagnostic clue*, not a treatment.

### What NOT to Do (Common Pitfalls)
- **Don’t call it “benign essential tremor”** — it can be severely disabling.
- **Don’t use propranolol in asthma** — can cause fatal bronchospasm.
- **Don’t start primidone at high dose** — first-dose phenomenon (confusion, ataxia, vomiting) is common; start 50 mg nocte.
- **Don’t use levodopa** — it won’t work and delays appropriate therapy.
- **Don’t undertake extensive imaging** for a classic presentation.
- **Don’t forget to ask about family history and alcohol effect** — these are the strongest pattern-recognition cues.

---

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Essential Tremor | Parkinson’s Disease | Dystonic Tremor | Enhanced Physiologic Tremor | Cerebellar Tremor |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|------------------|-----------------------------|-------------------|
| **Pain** | None | Only if rigidity (aching) or dystonia | Often painful (cervical dystonia) | None | None |
| **Exam** | Action tremor only; no bradykinesia, rigidity, or gait disturbance | Rest tremor (pill-rolling), bradykinesia, rigidity, shuffling gait | Focal tremor at 6–8 Hz; directional (null point); dystonic posturing (head tilt, torticollis) | Fine 8–12 Hz postural; other signs of anxiety (tachycardia, hyperhidrosis) | Intention tremor (finger-to-nose worsens near target); dysmetria, ataxic gait, nystagmus, scanning speech |
| **ECG** | Normal (unless beta-blocker effects) | Normal | Normal | Sinus tachycardia | Normal |
| **Labs** | All normal | Normal | Normal | TSH, electrolytes normal; consider cortisol, caffeine | MRI shows cerebellar lesion (stroke, MS, tumour) |
| **Key Diff** | **Improves with alcohol** | No alcohol improvement; dopamine deficiency on DaTSCAN | May improve with touching the “null point” (e.g., placing a finger on the chin reduces head tremor) | Reversible when trigger removed | Structural lesion on imaging; other cerebellar signs |
| **Tremor Type** | Postural + kinetic (4–12 Hz) | Resting (3–6 Hz), but can have re-emergent postural tremor | Variable – often task-specific (e.g., writer’s cramp) | Postural (fine) | Intention (3–5 Hz) |

**Memory aid:** Think “**PARDON**” — **P**arkinson (rest), **A**ction (essential), **R**eversible (physiologic), **D**ystonic (directional), **O**ther signs (cerebellar with ataxia), **N**ormal exam (essential is pure tremor).

---

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Missed diagnosis leads to:**

1. **Functional disability** – inability to eat without spilling, write legibly, use tools. Leads to social withdrawal and loss of independence. **What to do:** Screen for functional impact with simple questions: “Can you drink from a cup without spilling? Can you sign your name?” Offer treatment.
2. **Falls** – Intention tremor can cause loss of balance when reaching for objects, especially climbing stairs or carrying items. Secondary fractures. **What to do:** Assess fall risk (get up and go test), refer to physio for balance exercises.
3. **Depression and social anxiety** – Embarrassment leads to avoidance of public eating/drinking. **What to do:** Ask about mood; refer to psychology; consider SSRIs (but be aware they can worsen tremor in some patients).
4. **Misdiagnosis as Parkinson’s disease** – Receiving inappropriate levodopa can cause side effects (nausea, dyskinesia, hallucinations in elderly) and delay correct management. Emotional harm of being told you have a neurodegenerative disease. **What to do:** Always insist on the presence of bradykinesia or rigidity before labelling PD. If uncertain, refer to movement disorder specialist.
5. **Late-stage cognitive decline** – Long-term studies show a slightly increased risk of dementia (OR ~1.5–2). Unlike PD, dementia is not a core feature, but cerebellar cognitive affective syndrome (CCAS) can occur. **Pattern:** executive dysfunction, verbal fluency problems, emotional lability. **What to do:** Screen with MoCA; refer to neurology if cognitive complaints.

---

## 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

### Mnemonics

1. **“ET: Exaggerated Tremor of the Arms – Check Head & Voice”** — reminds you that head and voice involvement are supportive of ET (though not required). If head tremor is the only manifestation, consider dystonia.

2. **“A-B-C-D”** for differentiating ET vs Parkinson’s:
   - **A**ction | **B**ilateral | **C**oarse (intention) → **E**ssential
   - **A**kinesia (bradykinesia) | **B**ent posture | **C**ogwheel rigidity | **D**ecreased arm swing → Parkinson’s

3. **“ALCOHOL IMPROVES”** – Not a mnemonic, but the most valuable pattern-recognition phrase to teach juniors. If you hear this, think ET until proven otherwise.

### Clinical Pearls (Pattern-Recognition Cues)

1. **“If the tremor stops when the patient is distracted, it’s psychogenic.”** True psychogenic tremor shows entrainment (tremor frequency matches another repetitive movement, e.g., tapping). ET does not entrain.
2. **“A patient with hand tremor who can still write a straight line with barely a wobble probably has Parkinson’s, not ET.”** PD handwriting is small (micrographia) but not tremulous; ET handwriting is large, shaky, and jagged.
3. **“The ‘water test’:** Ask the patient to carry a full cup of water across the room. In ET, water spills. In PD, they may lean forward, take short steps, and the cup is stable at rest – but they may spill due to freezing.
4. **“Propranolol works better for hand than head/voice tremor.”** For head and voice, consider primidone or botulinum toxin.
5. **“Isolated chin tremor in young adults is usually physiologic or essential, but never put a label on it until you’ve seen a neurologist.”** Reassure but review.
6. **“Don’t screen for Wilson’s disease in every patient >40 years old with tremor.”** Wilson’s presents before age 30; ceruloplasmin and copper testing outside this age is low yield unless other signs present.
7. **“If tremor is unilateral, think structural lesion first, until proven otherwise.”** Do brain MRI before labelling as ET.

### Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid

- **Calling early PD “essential tremor”** — missing bradykinesia (e.g., reduced arm swing, hypomimia) is the most common error. Always check rapid finger tapping, foot tapping, and gait.
- **Prescribing propranolol without checking heart rate and asthma history** — can cause syncope, bronchospasm. Get baseline ECG if >65 years or history of cardiac disease.
- **Dismissing tremor as “just stress”** — older adults often present late; they may have suffered for years. Take it seriously.
- **Starting primidone at 250 mg daily** — leads to “first-dose phenomenon” (severe dizziness, ataxia). Start low (50 mg nocte) and go slow.

---

## 10. Escalation Criteria

Essential tremor is not a medical emergency. However, you need to know when the situation is **not** simple ET.

### When to Call Senior / On-Call Neurologist

- **Rapid onset** (days to weeks) — possible stroke, demyelination, drug toxicity, or psychogenic.
- **New neurological signs** – e.g., hemiparesis, ataxia, dysarthria, nystagmus, visual field deficits. This could be cerebellar stroke or tumour.
- **Rest tremor** + bradykinesia or rigidity – suggests Parkinson’s disease; refer to neurologist for initiation of dopamine therapy.
- **Tremor causing acute injury** – e.g., fall with head strike. Manage fall; then address tremor long-term.
- **Patient on anticoagulation with new head tremor** – consider cerebellar haemorrhage; urgent CT head.

### When to Call Crash Team / ICU

- **Extreme tremor causing respiratory compromise** –
